title: Building Systems & IS Ethics
simple_html:
This lesson covers two linked ideas: how organizations build information systems, and the ethical responsibilities that come with running them. Building a system is rarely just coding — it is a structured process of figuring out what is needed, designing it, building it, and keeping it running.

How systems get built

The classic approach is the Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC), with stages roughly: define the problem, gather requirements, design, build, test, deploy, and maintain. Two broad styles exist:

  • Waterfall — do each stage fully before the next. Predictable, but slow to change.
  • Agile — build in small, fast cycles, getting feedback often. Flexible, better for uncertain needs.

Other options include buying ready-made software, using prototyping (quick mock-ups to learn), and outsourcing or cloud services. Most large IT projects fail or overrun, usually because of poor requirements, weak user involvement, or change resistance — not bad code.

Why ethics matters

Information systems give organizations huge power over data about people. That creates duties. Key ethical issues include:

  • Privacy — collecting and using personal data responsibly.
  • Accuracy — who is responsible when data or a system is wrong?
  • Property — respecting intellectual property like software and content.
  • Accountability — who answers when a system causes harm?

Key takeaways

  • Building systems is a managed process, not just programming.
  • Agile vs. waterfall is a core trade-off: flexibility vs. predictability.
  • Most IT projects fail for people and requirements reasons.
  • Ethics is built in, not bolted on — especially around privacy.
intermediate_html:
This lesson combines the practice of building information systems with the professional and ethical obligations of those who build and operate them. You should be able to describe the development life cycle, compare development approaches, explain why projects fail, and analyze ethical dilemmas systematically.

The Systems Development Life Cycle

The SDLC structures system building into phases: systems analysis (define the problem and gather requirements), systems design (specify how the solution will meet them), programming, testing (unit, system, and acceptance), conversion (cutover to the new system), and production and maintenance. Requirements gathering is the most consequential step: errors caught here are cheap, but errors found after deployment can cost orders of magnitude more to fix.

Comparing development approaches

ApproachBest whenTrade-off
Waterfall (SDLC)Requirements are stable and clearPredictable but rigid and slow
Agile / iterativeRequirements evolve; fast feedback neededFlexible but needs engaged users and discipline
PrototypingRequirements are uncertainFast learning, risk of "good enough" hacks
Packaged / SaaSNeed is common, not uniqueFast and cheap but limited customization
Outsourcing / offshoreLacking internal capacityCost savings vs. coordination and control risk

Agile and DevOps push small, frequent, automated releases with continuous integration, contrasting with waterfall's big upfront design and single large release.

Why projects fail and how to manage them

A large share of IT projects run over budget, late, or fail to deliver value. The causes are mostly organizational: vague or shifting requirements, weak user involvement, inadequate change management, poor project governance, and unrealistic estimates. Business process reengineering (Hammer & Champy) reminds us that automating a broken process just makes it fail faster — redesign the process first. Success factors include strong sponsorship, user participation, realistic scope, and managing the people side of change.

A framework for ethical analysis

When a system raises an ethical question, a five-step process helps: (1) identify the facts clearly; (2) define the conflict and the higher-order values at stake; (3) identify the stakeholders; (4) identify reasonable options; (5) identify the consequences of each. Candidate decisions can be tested against ethical principles such as the Golden Rule, Kant's Categorical Imperative (would it be acceptable if everyone did it?), the Utilitarian principle (greatest good), and the slippery-slope rule.

The core moral dimensions

Mason's framework (often summarized as PAPA) highlights Privacy, Accuracy, Property, and Accessibility. Modern practice adds accountability and algorithmic fairness. Privacy dominates: regulations like the GDPR grant individuals rights over their data (consent, access, erasure) and impose heavy penalties, making privacy a design requirement, not an afterthought.

Worked example: a recommendation feature

A product team wants to use customers' purchase history to power personalized recommendations. Ethical analysis: the value conflict is personalization (utility, revenue) versus privacy and consent. Stakeholders are customers, the firm, and regulators. Options range from opt-out tracking to explicit opt-in with transparent controls. Testing against the Categorical Imperative and GDPR's consent requirement favors clear opt-in and data minimization — which is also the lower-risk legal choice. The example shows ethics and good engineering converging.

How to apply it

  • Choose a development approach by matching requirement stability to method.
  • Treat project risk as mostly organizational; invest in users and change management.
  • Run any data-sensitive feature through the five-step ethical analysis and PAPA lens.
  • Bake privacy and accountability into design from the start.
full_html:
This capstone lesson unites two themes that bracket the life of any information system: the disciplined practice of building systems, and the ethical and professional responsibilities that govern how systems are designed, deployed, and used. The pairing is deliberate — the choices made during development (what data to collect, how to test, who is accountable) are precisely where ethical obligations become concrete design decisions.

The Systems Development Life Cycle

The SDLC remains the foundational model: systems analysis (problem definition, feasibility study, requirements determination), systems design (logical and physical specifications), programming, testing (unit, system, and user acceptance), conversion (direct cutover, parallel, pilot, or phased), and production and maintenance. Requirements analysis is decisive: Boehm's (1981) cost-of-change curve shows defect-correction cost rising roughly an order of magnitude at each later phase, justifying heavy early investment in getting requirements right. Structured methods model the system before building it — data-flow diagrams and entity-relationship models for the data, and increasingly object-oriented analysis and UML.

Development approaches and their trade-offs

The classic waterfall SDLC sequences phases and suits stable, well-understood requirements but resists change and defers value delivery. Prototyping builds quick working models to elicit requirements when needs are unclear. Packaged software and modern SaaS trade customization for speed and lower total cost of ownership. End-user development and low-code platforms shift building toward business users. Outsourcing and offshoring trade cost for coordination and control risk. The dominant contemporary paradigm is agile (Beck et al., Agile Manifesto, 2001), which delivers working software in short iterations with continuous user feedback, embracing change rather than freezing scope; DevOps extends this with continuous integration and delivery, automated testing, and tight dev-operations collaboration to release frequently and reliably. The strategic rule is to match method to context: the greater the requirement uncertainty and need for adaptability, the more iterative the approach should be.

Process change and why projects fail

Building systems often means changing how the organization works. Business process reengineering (Hammer, 1990; Hammer & Champy, 1993) argues for radical redesign of processes rather than automating existing ones — "don't pave the cow paths." More incremental approaches include continuous improvement and business process management. Yet a large share of IT projects fail, are late, or overrun budgets. The Standish Group's CHAOS reports and decades of research locate the causes overwhelmingly in the organizational dimension: poor or volatile requirements, inadequate user involvement, weak executive sponsorship, poor estimation and scope creep, and resistance to change (Markus, 1983, on power and resistance). Mitigation centers on user participation, realistic scoping, strong governance, and explicit change management — the people side rather than the technical side. (This connects back to the implementation-risk themes from the Enterprise Applications lesson.)

The ethical dimension: why systems create obligations

Information systems concentrate power over data and decisions, generating duties toward those affected. Mason's (1986) influential PAPA framework names four moral dimensions: Privacy (control over personal information), Accuracy (who is responsible for the fidelity of data and outputs), Property (ownership of information and intellectual property), and Accessibility (who may obtain what information). Laudon and Laudon broaden this to five dimensions including information rights, property rights, accountability and control, system quality, and quality of life. Three principles underlie analysis: responsibility (accepting the costs and duties of one's decisions), accountability (mechanisms to determine who is responsible), and liability (legal recourse for harm).

A structured method for ethical analysis

A repeatable five-step process disciplines judgment: (1) identify and describe the facts; (2) define the conflict or dilemma and the higher-order values in tension; (3) identify the stakeholders; (4) identify the options that can reasonably be taken; (5) identify the potential consequences of each option. Candidate actions are then tested against established ethical principles: the Golden Rule; Kant's Categorical Imperative (act only as you would have everyone act); Descartes' rule of change / slippery slope (if an action cannot be repeated indefinitely, it should not be taken at all); the Utilitarian Principle (choose the greatest good); the Risk Aversion Principle (avoid actions with catastrophic potential cost); and the no-free-lunch rule (assume things have owners). These map to deontological versus consequentialist traditions in moral philosophy.

Contemporary issues: privacy, IP, and algorithmic accountability

Privacy is the dominant practical concern. Frameworks such as Fair Information Practices and regulations including the EU's GDPR and the CCPA grant data subjects rights (notice, consent, access, portability, erasure) and mandate privacy by design and data minimization, backed by severe penalties. Intellectual property regimes — copyright, patents, and trade secrets — protect software and content but strain under digital copying and AI training data. The rise of machine learning adds algorithmic accountability: opaque models can encode bias, raising questions of fairness, explainability, and who is liable when an automated decision causes harm.

Worked example: deploying a hiring algorithm

A firm considers an ML model to screen resumes. Step 1, facts: the model is trained on historical hiring data. Step 2, conflict: efficiency and consistency versus fairness, accuracy, and privacy. Step 3, stakeholders: applicants, employees, the firm, regulators. Step 4, options: deploy as-is, deploy with bias auditing and human review, or reject. Step 5, consequences: training on biased history risks perpetuating discrimination (an accuracy and fairness failure with legal liability). Tested against the Categorical Imperative and Risk Aversion, the defensible path is auditing for disparate impact, retaining human oversight, documenting accountability, and ensuring data-use consent — again showing ethical and sound engineering practice converge.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Believing system building is mainly programming — analysis, testing, and change management dominate.
  • Treating agile as "no planning" rather than disciplined, iterative planning.
  • Automating a flawed process instead of reengineering it first.
  • Attributing project failure to technology when the root cause is usually organizational.
  • Treating ethics and privacy as compliance to bolt on at the end rather than design requirements.
  • Assuming an algorithm is neutral — it inherits the biases of its training data.

Cross-links

This lesson draws on Security & Control (system quality and privacy as design requirements), Enterprise Applications (implementation and change-management risk), and Data & Business Intelligence (governance and accuracy of the data that systems process). As the course capstone, it reframes every prior technical topic as a set of choices with managerial and moral consequences.
